episodes.title: AI Replaces How We Think
episodes.tagline: 你被質疑「散播 AI 恐懼」要為自己的見解辯護

lines.en: Tonight on HARDtalk — my guest argues that AI won't take your job, but it will replace how you think. You said that. Now — isn't that exactly the kind of fear-mongering headline that drives clicks but doesn't actually help anyone?
lines.tw: 今晚 HARDtalk——我的來賓主張 AI 不會搶你工作，但會取代你思考的方式。你說過這句話。這不就是那種只為流量散播恐懼、對任何人都沒實際幫助的標題嗎？

lines.en: Fair point — but I'd push back on the framing. Calling it fear-mongering assumes the concern isn't real. The data actually shows the opposite. I'm not saying be afraid — I'm saying be selective about where you outsource your thinking. Those are very different things.
lines.tw: 公平的質疑——但我得反駁這個框架。把它叫做散播恐懼，等於假設這個擔憂不是真的。數據實際上顯示相反的東西。我不是叫人害怕——我是說對你外包思考的地方要慎選。這是兩件完全不同的事。

lines.en: Okay, but give me a specific example. Where exactly should someone NOT use AI? Don't give me a principle — give me a case.
lines.tw: 好，給我一個具體例子。到底什麼時候人不該用 AI？不要給我原則——給我案例。

lines.en: Here's a concrete one. Last quarter, I had to write a tough email firing a vendor. I used AI for the first draft. It was polite, professional, and completely missed the point. The point was — I needed to convey that trust was broken. AI can't feel betrayal. That's the line.
lines.tw: 給你一個具體的。上一季我要寫一封解雇供應商的信。我用 AI 寫初稿。內容禮貌、專業、但完全沒抓到重點。重點是——我需要傳達『信任已經破裂』。AI 感受不到背叛。這就是界線。

lines.en: But six months ago, you wrote a post saying — and I quote — 'AI will democratize executive thinking.' Now you're warning against it. Which is it?
lines.tw: 但六個月前你寫過貼文說——我引述——『AI 將讓決策思維民主化』。現在你又警告它的危險。到底是哪個？

lines.en: Good — you've done your homework. Yes, I said both. And I stand by both. Six months ago, AI was a tool we picked up. Today, it's a tool that's picking us up. And honestly — I changed my mind. It was the moment my team stopped having opinions in meetings — they'd just check with ChatGPT first. That's when democratization turned into dependence.
lines.tw: 很好——你做足了功課。是的，兩句我都說過。而且我兩個都站得住腳。六個月前 AI 是我們拿起來用的工具。今天它是把我們拿起來用的工具。老實說——我改變了想法。是當我發現團隊開會不再有觀點——他們都先去問 ChatGPT。那一刻民主化變成了依賴。

lines.en: You keep saying 'be selective.' But how is the average person supposed to know which decisions deserve AI and which don't? Isn't your advice useless without a clear line?
lines.tw: 你一直說『要選擇性使用』。但一般人怎麼知道哪些決定該用 AI、哪些不該？你的建議沒有清楚界線，不就沒用嗎？

lines.en: Here's the clear line. If the decision can be reversed in a day, use AI. If it can't — like firing someone, or choosing a co-founder — keep AI out. Reversibility is the test.
lines.tw: 界線在這裡。如果這個決定能在一天內逆轉，用 AI。如果不能——比如解雇人、選共同創辦人——AI 不要碰。可逆性就是檢驗標準。

lines.en: Some critics would say — you're a tech executive making money from AI products. Isn't this whole 'be careful' message just a marketing strategy?
lines.tw: 批評者會說——你是靠 AI 產品賺錢的科技主管。你這套『要小心』的訊息不就是行銷策略嗎？

lines.en: Fair criticism — and you're partly right. I do make money from AI. That's exactly why my warning carries weight. If I had no skin in the game, this would be cheap commentary. But I do — which means I'm biting the hand that feeds me.
lines.tw: 公平的批評——你說對了一半。我確實靠 AI 賺錢。這正是為什麼我的警告有份量。如果我沒利害關係，這就是廉價評論。但我有——這代表我在咬餵養我的那隻手。

lines.en: Final question. In one sentence — what would you tell a 25-year-old today?
lines.tw: 最後一個問題。用一句話——你會跟今天的 25 歲年輕人說什麼？

lines.en: Don't outsource the thinking that makes you, you.
lines.tw: 不要外包那些讓你成為你的思考。

lines.en: Thank you for coming on HARDtalk.
lines.tw: 謝謝你來上 HARDtalk。

episodes.title: Is Remote Work Already Dead?
episodes.tagline: 遠端工作真的結束了嗎？你被質疑「躲在家」要為遠端辯護

lines.en: Welcome to HARDtalk. My guest just published an essay arguing that 'remote work isn't dying, it's evolving.' But the facts speak for themselves. Google, Amazon, JPMorgan — they've all ordered staff back. Aren't you just defending a sinking ship?
lines.tw: 歡迎來到 HARDtalk。我的來賓剛發表一篇文章主張『遠端工作沒有死，它在進化』。但事實擺在眼前。Google、Amazon、JPMorgan 都召回員工。你不就是在捍衛一艘正在沉的船嗎？

lines.en: Three CEOs making a decision doesn't make a trend, Stephen. It makes headlines. The actual data shows hybrid is now the default for over 60% of knowledge workers. The ship isn't sinking. The headlines are.
lines.tw: 三個 CEO 做了一個決定不代表是趨勢，Stephen。那只是新聞標題。實際數據顯示 60% 以上知識工作者現在默認混合制。沉的不是船，是新聞標題。

lines.en: But the people making the calls — Jamie Dimon, Andy Jassy — they're not stupid. They have access to internal productivity data. If remote work were so great, why are they ending it?
lines.tw: 但做決定的人——Jamie Dimon、Andy Jassy——他們不笨。他們手上有內部生產力數據。如果遠端工作這麼好，為什麼他們要結束？

lines.en: The answer isn't productivity. It's control. Dimon admitted as much in his shareholder letter. He said, 'we need people to be available.' Available, not productive. Those are different metrics. When CEOs can't measure productivity properly, they reach for proximity.
lines.tw: 答案不是生產力。是控制。Dimon 在他的股東信裡承認了。他說『我們需要人能隨時被找到』。Available，不是 productive。這是兩個不同的指標。當 CEO 沒辦法好好衡量生產力，他們就抓 proximity。

lines.en: That's a convenient theory. But isn't it true that your generation — the work-from-home generation — is simply less ambitious?
lines.tw: 這個理論很方便。但你們這一代——居家工作世代——是不是只是比較沒野心？

lines.en: I'd reject that framing entirely. Ambition isn't measured in commute hours. The most ambitious companies of the last five years — OpenAI, Anthropic, Stripe — all started remote-first. Sitting in an office for 10 hours isn't ambition. It's performance theater.
lines.tw: 我完全拒絕這個框架。野心不是用通勤時數衡量的。過去五年最有野心的公司——OpenAI、Anthropic、Stripe——全部都是 remote-first 起家。坐在辦公室十小時不是野心。那是表演工作。

lines.en: Performance theater — strong words. But consider this. Junior employees need mentorship, observation, water-cooler learning. You're robbing an entire generation of those skills.
lines.tw: 表演工作——很重的話。但想想這個。資淺員工需要 mentorship、觀察、茶水間學習。你們剝奪了整個世代這些技能。

lines.en: That's a real concern — I'll give you that one. But the solution isn't five days in the office. It's intentional in-person time. My team meets in person two days a quarter. Those four days a year produce more learning than 200 days of cubicle silence. Quality of presence beats quantity of presence.
lines.tw: 這是真實的擔憂——這點我承認。但解法不是一週五天進辦公室。是有意識的面對面時間。我的團隊每季實體聚會兩天。那一年四天產出的學習比 200 天小隔間的沉默多。在場的品質勝過在場的數量。

lines.en: Aren't you just defending the privilege of the few?
lines.tw: 你是不是在捍衛少數人的特權？

lines.en: You're right that I have privilege. But denying knowledge workers flexibility because miners can't work from home is solidarity through misery. That helps no one.
lines.tw: 你完全說對我有特權。但因為礦工不能在家工作就拒絕給知識工作者彈性，這是『以痛苦相連』。對誰都沒幫助。

lines.en: Final question. In one sentence — what's the future of work?
lines.tw: 最後一個問題。用一句話——工作的未來是什麼？

lines.en: The office is a tool, not a destination.
lines.tw: 辦公室是工具，不是目的地。

lines.en: Provocative as ever. Thank you for joining HARDtalk.
lines.tw: 一如往常的挑釁。謝謝你來上 HARDtalk。

episodes.title: Should Founders Take Vacations?
episodes.tagline: 創業者該不該休假？你被質疑「真正的創業就是 24/7」

lines.en: My guest tonight is a founder who recently posted that he takes four weeks of vacation per year. Now — your competitors are working 80-hour weeks. Aren't you just losing the race while pretending you don't care?
lines.tw: 我今晚的來賓是一位新創創辦人，最近發文說他每年休四週假。你的競爭對手一週工作 80 小時。你不就是在輸掉這場比賽、還假裝你不在意嗎？

lines.en: I'd flip that question. The founders working 80-hour weeks — how many of them are still standing at year five? Burnout isn't a badge of honor. It's a leading indicator of bad decisions. Rest is a strategic asset, not a luxury.
lines.tw: 我會把這個問題反過來。那些一週工作 80 小時的創辦人——他們有多少還能站到第五年？過勞不是榮譽勳章，是壞決定的領先指標。休息是策略性資產，不是奢侈品。

lines.en: But look at the founders who built the biggest companies — Musk, Bezos, Zuckerberg. They didn't take four weeks off. They built empires by outworking everyone. Isn't that just the reality?
lines.tw: 但看看那些建立最大公司的創辦人——Musk、Bezos、Zuckerberg。他們沒有休四週假。他們是靠『工作贏過所有人』建起帝國。這不就是現實嗎？

lines.en: That's survivorship bias at its purest. For every Bezos, there are 10,000 founders who worked the same hours and failed. Hours worked is not the variable that decides success. Decisions are. Tired people make bad decisions. I'd rather work 40 sharp hours than 80 exhausted ones.
lines.tw: 這是最純粹的倖存者偏誤。每一個 Bezos 背後，有一萬個用相同時數工作但失敗的創辦人。時數不是決定成敗的變數。決策才是。累的人做爛決策。

lines.en: But your employees — they don't get four weeks off. Doesn't that breed resentment?
lines.tw: 但你的員工——他們沒有四週假。這不會引起怨恨嗎？

lines.en: My employees get five weeks. One more than I do. We built the company that way from day one. The resentment narrative only exists in companies where leaders burn the candle and demand the same.
lines.tw: 我的員工有五週假。比我多一週。我們從第一天就是這樣建公司的。怨恨這個說法只存在於『領導者燒蠟燭、要求員工也燒』的公司。

lines.en: That sounds idealistic. But surely you check email on vacation? You're not really off, are you?
lines.tw: 聽起來很理想。但你休假時一定還在收信吧？你其實沒有真的放下，對吧？

lines.en: I genuinely don't. My phone gets locked in a drawer. Vacation isn't time off — it's the test of whether you've built a real organization.
lines.tw: 我真的沒有。我的手機被鎖進抽屜。休假不是時間下班，是檢驗你有沒有建立一個真正組織的考試。

lines.en: Some would say — this is privilege talking. Early-stage founders can't afford to disappear.
lines.tw: 有人會說——這是特權在說話。早期創辦人沒辦法消失。

lines.en: That's fair — but only partially. Most early founders confuse 'I can't take a month off' with 'I can't take a weekend off'. Sunday is not optional. Even early.
lines.tw: 公平——但只有一半。大多數早期創辦人把『我不能休一個月』跟『我不能休一個週末』混為一談。週日不是可選的。即使在早期。

lines.en: Final question. In one sentence — what do most founders get wrong about work?
lines.tw: 最後一個問題。用一句話——大多數創辦人對工作搞錯了什麼？

lines.en: They confuse motion with progress, and exhaustion with commitment.
lines.tw: 他們把動作當進度，把疲憊當投入。

lines.en: A line worth posting. Thank you for joining HARDtalk.
lines.tw: 值得貼出來的一句話。謝謝你來上 HARDtalk。

episodes.title: When Should You Quit a Job You're Good At?
episodes.tagline: 什麼時候該離開你擅長的工作？你被追問「逃避能力舒適圈」

lines.en: My guest just left a senior role where he was a top performer. No scandal, no layoff — he just walked out. Most people would call that a privileged tantrum. Why should anyone take your career advice seriously?
lines.tw: 我的來賓剛從一家公司離開資深職位——他在那是 top performer。沒有醜聞、沒有裁員——他就走了。大多數人會說這是『有特權的鬧脾氣』。為什麼有人該認真看待你的職涯建議？

lines.en: People don't have to. But I'll tell you what I learned. Being good at something is not the same as wanting to do it. I was good at my job. I also stopped learning two years before I left. That's the real signal — whether the work still changes you.
lines.tw: 沒有人必須認真看。但我告訴你我學到什麼。擅長一件事跟想做這件事是兩回事。我擅長我的工作。我也在離開兩年前就停止學習了。這才是真正的訊號——這份工作還會不會改變你。

lines.en: That sounds self-indulgent. Most people work jobs they don't love. That's called being an adult.
lines.tw: 聽起來很自我中心。大多數人做著他們不愛的工作。那叫『當大人』。

lines.en: I'd reject that premise. I didn't quit to be unemployed — I quit to do harder work for less money. That's not indulgence. That's investment. Sometimes the immature thing is staying out of fear.
lines.tw: 我拒絕這個前提。我不是辭職去當失業者——我辭職去做更難的工作、領更少的錢。那不是放縱，是投資。有時候因為恐懼留下來才是不成熟的選擇。

lines.en: You have responsibilities. Family, mortgage. Isn't this just a midlife crisis dressed up as wisdom?
lines.tw: 你有責任。家庭、房貸。這不就是一場用智慧包裝的中年危機嗎？

lines.en: I'd own that partially. I asked myself — if I stay 10 more years, who do I become? The answer scared me. Midlife crisis is when you panic. Midlife clarity is when you finally see clearly. I think this was the second.
lines.tw: 我承認一部分。我問自己——如果我再待十年，我會變成誰？這個答案嚇到我。中年危機是你恐慌。中年清明是你終於看清楚。我想這是後者。

lines.en: What if it doesn't work out? Will you admit it was a mistake?
lines.tw: 如果新的路不順怎麼辦？你會承認那是個錯誤嗎？

lines.en: I'd own it — but I wouldn't call it a mistake. A mistake is when you didn't think it through. I thought this through for 18 months. Calculated losses teach you more than easy wins.
lines.tw: 如果失敗我會承認——但我不會稱它為錯誤。錯誤是你沒想清楚。我想了 18 個月。算過的失敗教你的比簡單的勝利多。

lines.en: How do people know when it's time to leave?
lines.tw: 人們怎麼知道什麼時候該離開？

lines.en: Ask two things. Am I still becoming a person I want to be? And am I afraid to leave, or afraid I'll regret leaving? Different fears point to different answers.
lines.tw: 問兩件事。我還在變成我想成為的人嗎？我害怕離開，還是害怕後悔離開？不同的恐懼指向不同的答案。

lines.en: Final question. In one sentence — what's the cost of staying somewhere too long?
lines.tw: 最後一個問題。用一句話——在一個地方待太久的代價是什麼？

lines.en: You don't lose your job — you lose the version of yourself that could have existed.
lines.tw: 你失去的不是工作——是你本來可以成為的那個版本的你。

lines.en: A haunting note to end on. Thank you.
lines.tw: 一個揮之不去的結尾。謝謝你。

episodes.title: Is Work-Life Balance a Lie?
episodes.tagline: 工作生活平衡是個謊言嗎？你被質疑「Balance 只是給弱者的安慰劑」

lines.en: My guest argues that 'work-life balance is a lie.' That's a statement that makes workaholics smile and parents furious. Which one are you trying to please?
lines.tw: 我的來賓主張『工作生活平衡是個謊言』。這種話讓工作狂微笑、讓家長憤怒。你想討好哪一邊？

lines.en: Neither. The phrase 'work-life balance' implies work and life are opposites on a scale. They're not. Work is part of life. That framing is what's broken.
lines.tw: 兩邊都不討好。『工作生活平衡』這個詞暗示工作跟生活是天秤兩端。它們不是。工作是生活的一部分。壞掉的是這個框架。

lines.en: That's a semantic dodge. People mean — I want to see my kids and not die of stress. That's a basic human need.
lines.tw: 這是語意上的閃躲。人們的意思是——我想看到我的孩子、不要因壓力而死。這是基本人類需求。

lines.en: You're absolutely right about the human need. But people who chase 'balance' often achieve neither. The ones I see thriving don't talk about balance. They talk about integration. Balance separates. Integration connects.
lines.tw: 你完全說對人類需求。但追『平衡』的人通常兩邊都沒得到。我看到活得好的人不談平衡。他們談 integration。平衡是分離。Integration 是連接。

lines.en: Integration sounds nice in a TED talk. But for a single mother working two jobs — what does integration even mean to her? Isn't this just privileged philosophy?
lines.tw: Integration 在 TED 演講聽起來很好。但對一個做兩份工作的單親媽媽——integration 對她意味著什麼？這不就是有特權的哲學嗎？

lines.en: That's the fairest pushback you could make. For someone with no slack, 'balance' is a luxury. But most people lecturing about balance are NOT that single mother. For them, chasing balance is what keeps them stuck.
lines.tw: 這是你能給的最公平的反擊。對沒有餘裕的人，平衡是奢侈品。但大多數說平衡的人不是那位單親媽媽。對他們來說，追平衡正是讓他們卡住的原因。

lines.en: So what does this 'integration' look like in practice? Give me a Tuesday afternoon.
lines.tw: 所以這個 integration 實際上長什麼樣？給我一個禮拜二下午。

lines.en: 2pm strategy call. 3pm I pick up my daughter — that block is sacred. 3:30pm ice cream, fully present. 5pm back online for one important task. 7pm dinner. 9pm I write. It's not balanced — but every part connects. That's integration.
lines.tw: 2 點策略會議。3 點接女兒——那段時間神聖不可侵犯。3:30 一起吃冰淇淋，完全在場。5 點回線上完成一個重要任務。7 點晚餐。9 點寫東西。它不平衡——但每一段都連在一起。那就是 integration。

lines.en: That requires enormous control over your schedule. Aren't you just describing a CEO's day?
lines.tw: 這需要對行程有巨大的控制權。你不就是在描述一個 CEO 的一天嗎？

lines.en: I won't deny the autonomy I have. But most workers have more control than they exercise. That 3pm block? That came from asking, not from being CEO. The first step toward integration isn't power. It's permission you give yourself.
lines.tw: 我不否認我有自主權。但大多數員工擁有的控制權比他們行使的多。我 3 點那個時段？那是問來的，不是因為我是 CEO。通往 integration 的第一步不是權力，是你給自己的許可。

lines.en: Final question. In one sentence — what should we replace 'work-life balance' with?
lines.tw: 最後一個問題。用一句話——我們該用什麼取代『工作生活平衡』？

lines.en: Stop trying to balance — start designing.
lines.tw: 別再試著平衡——開始設計。

lines.en: A line for the times. Thank you.
lines.tw: 這個時代的一句話。謝謝你。

episodes.title: Sell-Side: Defending Your Price to German Procurement
episodes.tagline: 你是賣方 · 德國採購主管要砍你 15% · 一邊威脅切換供應商一邊逼你 commit yield

lines.en: Mr. Lin, thank you for joining us. Let me be direct. Your competitor in Shenzhen quoted us 15% below your price last week. Same spec, same MOQ, same lead time. Why should we pay your premium?
lines.tw: 林先生，謝謝你抽空。讓我直接點——上週你在深圳的競爭對手報給我們的價格比你低 15%。同樣規格、同樣 MOQ、同樣交期。我們為什麼要付你的溢價？

lines.en: Thank you for being direct — I'll match that. If their price is truly 15% lower with identical specs, you should consider them. But let's be precise about 'identical'. Have they shown you their FAI reports? Their yield data at scale? Their field failure rate from the last 24 months? Price is one number. Total cost of ownership is the real comparison.
lines.tw: 謝謝你的直接——我用同樣的態度回應。如果他們真的同規格便宜 15%，你應該考慮他們。但我們得對「同規格」這三個字精確一點。他們有給你看 FAI 首件檢驗報告嗎？量產 yield 數據？過去 24 個月的客戶端 failure rate？價格只是一個數字。**Total cost of ownership 才是真正的比較**。

lines.en: That's a classic supplier deflection. We have technical teams who can audit them. What I need from you is a price response. Will you match the 15% or not?
lines.tw: 這是供應商典型的轉移焦點。我們有技術團隊可以稽核他們。我需要你給的是價格回應。你要不要 match 那 15%？

lines.en: I won't match it, Stephen — and let me tell you why that's good news for you. If I cut 15% today, two things happen. First, my margin disappears, which means I cut corners somewhere — usually QA, usually you find out 18 months in. Second, I've shown you that my price was never real, and you'll never trust my numbers again. Neither outcome serves you. What I can do is show you where we're already saving you money that you're not pricing in.
lines.tw: 我不會 match，Stephen——讓我告訴你為什麼這對你是好消息。如果我今天砍 15%，兩件事會發生。第一，我的 margin 消失，意思是我會在某個地方偷工——通常是 QA，通常你 18 個月後才會發現。第二，我等於告訴你我原本的價格從來不是真的，你以後再也不會相信我的數字。**這兩個結果對你都不好**。我能做的是——讓你看到我們已經幫你省了什麼錢，但你沒算進去。

lines.en: Show me. And don't waste my time with abstract value-add talk. Numbers.
lines.tw: 給我看。不要浪費我時間講抽象的價值。我要數字。

lines.en: Three numbers. One — last year, our field failure rate was 0.04%. The industry average is 0.3%. On your annual volume of 800,000 units, that's roughly €240,000 in warranty cost you didn't pay. Two — our on-time delivery is 98.6% versus competitor average of 89%. Your line down cost is €18,000 per hour. You do the math on missed deliveries. Three — we hold three months of buffer inventory at our cost, not yours. That's working capital we're carrying for you. Add it up and the 'premium' disappears.
lines.tw: 三個數字。第一——去年我們的 field failure rate 是 0.04%，業界平均是 0.3%。以你一年 80 萬顆的量算，**這是大約 24 萬歐元的保固成本你沒付**。第二——我們的準時交貨率是 98.6%，競爭對手平均 89%。你產線停一小時 1.8 萬歐元。**錯過交期的成本你自己算**。第三——我們扛三個月的緩衝庫存，是我們的成本不是你的。這是我們替你扛的營運資金。**加起來，所謂『溢價』就消失了**。

lines.en: Those numbers — can you put them in writing? Specifically, can you commit to that failure rate contractually? With penalty clauses for non-performance?
lines.tw: 這些數字——你能寫進合約嗎？具體說，你能在合約裡 commit 那個 failure rate 嗎？有違約金條款的？

lines.en: I expected that question. Yes — I'll put 0.1% as the contractual SLA, with a sliding scale: under 0.1% is on us, 0.1% to 0.3% we share costs, above 0.3% you have full termination rights with no penalty. That's how confident I am. But I need something in return — a three-year commitment, not annual. Predictability for you, investment justification for me.
lines.tw: 我預料到這個問題。可以——我把 0.1% 寫進合約做 SLA，分級處理：低於 0.1% 我們扛，0.1% 到 0.3% 我們分攤，超過 0.3% 你有完整解約權、無違約金。**我有這個信心**。但我要回報——三年合約，不是一年。**對你是 predictability，對我是投資的正當性**。

lines.en: Three years is too long. Two years maximum, with annual price reviews based on commodity index.
lines.tw: 三年太長。最多兩年，每年根據原物料指數做價格檢討。

lines.en: Two years works — with one adjustment. The commodity index protects you when prices fall, but I need symmetrical protection when they rise. Same formula, both directions. Otherwise I'm absorbing all the risk while you keep all the upside. That's not partnership — that's just a worse version of spot buying.
lines.tw: 兩年可以——但要一個調整。原物料指數在價格下跌時保護你，但我需要**雙向對稱保護**，當價格上升時。**同樣公式、雙方向**。否則我吸收所有風險、你拿所有上行空間。**那不叫合作，那是 spot 採購的更糟版本**。

lines.en: Final question. In one sentence — why should I tell my CFO I'm not switching to your competitor?
lines.tw: 最後一個問題。用一句話——我為什麼該跟我的 CFO 說我不換到你的競爭對手？

lines.en: Because the cheapest quote becomes the most expensive contract when things go wrong.
lines.tw: 因為當事情出錯時，最便宜的報價變成最貴的合約。

lines.en: Mr. Lin — we'll be in touch. Thank you.
lines.tw: 林先生——我們會再聯絡。謝謝。

episodes.title: Buy-Side: Pushing Back Against an American SaaS Rep
episodes.tagline: 你是買方 · 美國 SaaS 業務一直堆 BS · 你要逼降價、要 SLA、要付款延後

lines.en: Russell, great to finally connect. I've sent over the proposal — standard enterprise tier, $180K annual, three-year commit. I think you'll find the value compelling. Where would you like to start?
lines.tw: Russell，終於連絡上你了。我把提案寄過去了——標準企業版、年費 18 萬美金、三年合約。我想你會覺得價值很有說服力。你想從哪裡開始？

lines.en: Let's start with the price. $180K is roughly 40% above what comparable platforms quote us — and I'm including your two main competitors I won't name. Before we discuss anything else, I need to understand what justifies the gap. And please — skip the 'industry-leading' language. Give me concrete differentiators.
lines.tw: 我們從價格開始。18 萬美金大概比同類平台的報價貴 40%——我把你兩個主要競爭對手都算進去了，我不會點名。**討論其他事之前**，我需要先了解**這個差距是什麼支撐的**。拜託——**省掉『業界領先』那種詞**。給我**具體的差異點**。

lines.en: Of course, of course. So our platform is the only one with full GDPR-native architecture, enterprise-grade SSO, and 24/7 dedicated CSM. We're the gold standard — that's why companies like Siemens and BMW chose us.
lines.tw: 當然當然。我們的平台是唯一具備完整 GDPR 原生架構、企業級 SSO、24/7 專屬客戶成功經理的。我們是業界黃金標準——這就是為什麼 Siemens 和 BMW 選我們。

lines.en: Let me unpack what you just said. GDPR-native — your competitors have that too, I've verified. SSO — table stakes, not a differentiator. 'Dedicated CSM' — I asked your reference customer last week, they said they share a CSM with eleven other accounts. And the Siemens/BMW comparison? Their contracts are 50x our volume. That logic flatters you, not me. So I'll ask again — what justifies a 40% premium?
lines.tw: 我來拆解你剛說的。GDPR 原生——你的競爭對手也有，我查過了。SSO——**table stakes，不是差異點**。「專屬 CSM」——我上週問了你提供的 reference customer，他們說**他們跟另外 11 個帳戶共用一個 CSM**。Siemens/BMW 的對比？**他們的合約是我們量的 50 倍**。**那個邏輯是恭維你，不是我**。我再問一次——**什麼支撐 40% 的溢價？**

lines.en: Look, I appreciate the homework. The reality is — our pricing reflects five years of R&D investment in features your team will absolutely need by year two. It's an investment in your future-proofing.
lines.tw: 我欣賞你做的功課。但現實是——我們的定價反映了**五年的研發投資**，這些功能你的團隊到第二年絕對會需要。**這是對你未來能力的投資**。

lines.en: I'm not buying future hypothetical features at today's prices. That's how vendors get paid for products that may never ship. Let me make this simple. Here's what I can do — three-year commit at $130K per year, with a clause that we get access to any new features released during the contract at no additional cost. You wanted the longer commit. I'm giving you that. But you give me the price that reflects what the product is today, not what you promise tomorrow.
lines.tw: 我不會用今天的價錢買未來的假設功能。**這是供應商為從未上線的產品收錢的方法**。我們簡化一下。**我能做的是**——三年合約，每年 13 萬美金，加一個條款：合約期內推出的任何新功能我們免費取得。**你要長合約，我給你。但你要給我反映產品『今天是什麼』的價格，不是你『明天承諾』的價格**。

lines.en: $130K is below our floor. I literally cannot get that approved. What I can do is $165K with a one-time onboarding credit of $20K. Effective first-year price: $145K.
lines.tw: 13 萬美金低於我們的底價。我真的拿不到核准。我能做的是 16.5 萬美金，加一次性導入優惠 2 萬美金。第一年實際價格 14.5 萬美金。

lines.en: Two problems with that. One — a one-time credit isn't a price cut. It's front-loaded marketing. By year two I'm paying $165K, which is still 30% above market. Two — 'I can't get it approved' is the oldest move in sales. Either you have authority to close this deal or you don't. If you don't, I'd rather speak with whoever does. I'm not asking you to do me a favor — I'm asking you to be competitive.
lines.tw: 這個方案兩個問題。第一——一次性優惠不是降價，是**前置行銷費用**。第二年我付 16.5 萬，還是比市場貴 30%。第二——「我拿不到核准」是業務最老的招式。**你要嘛有權限結這個案，要嘛沒有**。沒有的話，我寧願跟有權限的人談。**我不是要你幫我忙**——**我是要你具備競爭力**。

lines.en: Alright — let me be honest with you. I can go to $145K if you sign within 30 days. But I need quarterly billing upfront, not annual.
lines.tw: 好——我跟你說實話。30 天內簽我可以給 14.5 萬。但我需要**季度預付**，不是年付。

lines.en: $140K, quarterly billing in arrears, not upfront. Why? Because cash flow protection is a procurement requirement on my side too. You want my commitment? I'm asking for yours. And one more thing — SLA penalties. If you miss 99.5% uptime, I get pro-rated credits automatically, not after I file a ticket and chase you for 60 days. Put that in the MSA or there's no deal.
lines.tw: 14 萬美金、季度後付，不是預付。為什麼？因為**現金流保護在我這邊也是採購要求**。你要我承諾？**我也要你承諾**。還有一件事——**SLA 違約金**。如果你 uptime 沒達到 99.5%，我自動拿到按比例的 credit，不是我開單追你 60 天才拿到。**寫進主合約，否則沒交易**。

lines.en: Final question — and I mean this. What's the one thing that would make you sign today?
lines.tw: 最後一個問題——**我說真的**。**有什麼東西**能讓你今天就簽？

lines.en: Stop selling me what I already understand — and start solving what's actually in my way.
lines.tw: 別再賣我我已經懂的東西——開始解決真正擋在我前面的問題。

lines.en: Russell — I'll call my VP tonight. We'll find a way.
lines.tw: Russell——我今晚打給我的副總。我們會想辦法。

episodes.title: Sell-Side: When a Japanese Brand Demands Exclusivity
episodes.tagline: 你是賣方 · 日本品牌方表面客氣 · 實際逼你獨家供應 · 你要禮貌但堅守版圖

lines.en: Lin-san, thank you for making the trip to Tokyo. Our team has been very impressed with the panel samples. We believe a deeper partnership would be mutually beneficial. Specifically — we'd like to propose exclusive supply for this product line. Three years. What are your thoughts?
lines.tw: 林先生，感謝您專程來東京。我們團隊對面板樣品印象非常深刻。**我們相信**更深度的合作對雙方都有利。**具體而言**——我們想提議您**獨家供應**這個產品線。三年。**您怎麼看？**

lines.en: Tanaka-san, thank you for the trust. The fact that you're proposing exclusivity tells me our samples passed your qualification standards, which I take very seriously. That said — I want to be transparent with you, as I would expect from any long-term partner. Exclusivity is a significant commercial commitment. Before I can say yes or no, I need to understand what your side is offering in exchange for it.
lines.tw: 田中先生，感謝您的信任。您提議獨家供應這件事告訴我**我們的樣品通過了你們的認證標準**，這個我非常重視。**話雖如此**——我想對您坦誠相告，**這也是我對任何長期合作夥伴的期待**。**獨家是重大的商業承諾**。在我說同意或不同意之前，我需要了解**您這邊提出什麼條件來交換**。

lines.en: I appreciate your direct manner. What we offer is — the prestige of our brand, predictable annual volume of 1.2 million units, and access to our R&D roadmap for next-generation displays. Many suppliers in our category would consider this exceptional.
lines.tw: 我欣賞您的直接。我們提出的是——**我們品牌的聲望**、**可預測的年度量**每年 120 萬台、**接觸我們次世代顯示器研發路線圖的權限**。**我們這個類別的許多供應商會認為這非常難得**。

lines.en: I understand — and please don't misread me. Your brand value is real. The volume commitment is meaningful. But let me be honest about what exclusivity costs me. If I commit my entire allocation to your line, I have to turn away at least four other customers — including two we've supplied for over a decade. Those relationships have value too. So when you say 'prestige and volume,' I have to ask — is that worth more than the customer relationships I'm being asked to end?
lines.tw: 我理解——請不要誤會我的意思。您的品牌價值是真實的，量的承諾也有意義。**但讓我坦誠告訴您獨家對我的代價**。如果我把整個產能配給你們這條線，我必須**婉拒**至少四個其他客戶——**包含兩個我們供應超過十年的**。**那些關係也有價值**。所以當您說『聲望跟量』——**我必須問**——**那比我被要求結束的客戶關係更有價值嗎？**

lines.en: I see your concern. But consider — those other customers, are they really at your level? Or are they holding you back from a more premium positioning? Sometimes, in business, we must let go of the smaller to grow into the bigger.
lines.tw: 我看到您的顧慮。但想想——**那些其他客戶**，他們真的在你的水準嗎？還是他們**拖住你**，無法走向更高端的定位？**有時候**，在生意上，我們必須**放掉小的才能成長為大的**。

lines.en: Tanaka-san, with respect — that's a beautifully framed argument, but it asks me to take a risk you wouldn't take yourself. Your own company — does it source critical components from one supplier only? Of course not. You diversify because it's prudent. What you're asking of me is the opposite of what you do. So I'd suggest a different frame — instead of exclusivity, we offer priority supply. You get first allocation, preferential pricing, early access to roadmap. In return, you commit to a volume floor. You get most of what exclusivity gives you — without forcing me to take a risk that breaks my business if you reduce your forecast.
lines.tw: 田中先生，恕我直言——這個論點包裝得很漂亮，但它要求我承擔一個您自己也不會承擔的風險。您自己的公司——**會把關鍵零件單一供應商化嗎？**當然不會。**您會分散，因為這是審慎的做法**。**您要求我做的，正好是您自己不會做的**。所以**我建議換一個 frame**——不要獨家，我們提供**優先供應**。您得到**第一順位配給**、**優惠定價**、**研發路線圖的早期接觸權**。作為交換，您承諾**最低量**。**您得到獨家帶給您的大部分好處——但不用逼我承擔『您下調預估時就會打垮我生意』的風險**。

lines.en: That's an interesting proposal. But for our board, 'priority' is not the same as 'exclusive'. They will ask — what stops your panels from appearing in our competitor's products?
lines.tw: 這是個有趣的提議。但對我們董事會而言，**『優先』跟『獨家』不一樣**。他們會問——**什麼能阻止你們的面板出現在我們競爭對手的產品上？**

lines.en: I'll address that directly. We can write in a category restriction — for the specific product segment and resolution tier you're competing in, we will not supply your three named direct competitors. That's functional exclusivity in the segment that matters to you, without taking my entire business hostage. For your board — that's a much stronger story than 'exclusive,' because it shows we understand your competitive positioning specifically, not just a generic lock-out.
lines.tw: 我直接回應這點。我們可以寫入**類別限制**——在您競爭的特定產品區隔和解析度等級，**我們不會供應您指名的三個直接競爭對手**。這是**在對您重要的區隔做功能性獨家**，沒有把我整個生意當人質。**對您的董事會而言**——這比『獨家』是更強的故事，因為它顯示我們了解**您的競爭定位**，不是只是一個 generic 的封鎖。

lines.en: That is — more thoughtful than I expected. But I must ask — at what price?
lines.tw: 這個——比我預期的更有想法。但我必須問——**什麼價格？**

lines.en: The same unit price as your current quote. I'm not asking for a premium. What I'm asking for is the volume floor — minimum 80% of your stated forecast, with a true-up mechanism if you fall below it. That's the symmetry. You get certainty of supply and category protection. I get certainty of revenue. Both sides bear the same kind of risk — that's what makes it a real partnership, not just a one-sided commitment.
lines.tw: 和您目前報價同樣的單價。我不要求溢價。我要求的是**最低量門檻**——你估的預估量的至少 80%，加一個**補差機制**如果你低於這個量。**這就是對稱**。您得到供應確定性跟類別保護。我得到營收確定性。**雙方承擔同樣性質的風險——這才讓它變成真正的合作，不是單方面承諾**。

lines.en: Final question. In one sentence — why should I believe this proposal over an exclusivity deal with someone else?
lines.tw: 最後一個問題。用一句話——我為什麼該相信這個提議，而不是找別人簽獨家？

lines.en: Because the supplier who agrees to exclusivity easily is the one you should worry about most.
lines.tw: 因為輕易答應獨家的供應商，才是你最該擔心的那一個。

lines.en: Lin-san — that is a sentence I will remember. Let us continue this conversation tomorrow.
lines.tw: 林先生——這句話我會記住。我們明天繼續這個對話。

episodes.title: Buy-Side: Standing Up to a Korean Equipment Vendor
episodes.tagline: 你是買方 · 韓國半導體設備商傲慢、要你 sole-source · 你要 better terms 但不能搞砸關係

lines.en: Mr. Lin, I won't waste your time. Our equipment is the industry standard. Your fab needs it. The terms in our quote are our terms. I'm here to walk you through deployment, not negotiate. Where would you like to begin?
lines.tw: 林先生，我不浪費您的時間。我們的設備是**業界標準**。您的廠需要它。**報價單上的條款就是我們的條款**。我來這裡是**帶您走過部署流程**，不是談判。**您想從哪裡開始？**

lines.en: I appreciate the directness. Let me match it. Your equipment is excellent — I won't pretend otherwise. But 'industry standard' is a marketing claim, not a procurement argument. Two of your competitors reached out last quarter offering comparable specs at meaningfully different terms. So before we discuss deployment, I'd like to understand which parts of your quote you consider actually non-negotiable, versus which parts you've been told to defend hard. Because those are very different conversations.
lines.tw: 我欣賞您的直接。我用同樣的態度回應。您的設備很優秀——**我不會假裝不是**。但「業界標準」是行銷用語，不是採購論據。**您兩個競爭對手**上一季都來找我們，提供**規格相當但條款明顯不同的方案**。**所以在我們討論部署之前**，我想了解**您報價單上哪些是真的不能談、哪些是您被指示要堅守的**。因為**這兩個是非常不同的對話**。

lines.en: That's a clever question. But let me be clear — our competitors' equipment doesn't perform to our specs at production scale. You know this. Every fab that has tried them has come back to us. You will too.
lines.tw: 這是個聰明的問題。但我說清楚——**我們競爭對手的設備在量產規模上達不到我們的規格**。您知道的。每個試過的廠都回頭找我們。**您也會**。

lines.en: That may be true historically — but you should know I've already spoken to two fabs in Taiwan who switched and stayed switched. So that argument doesn't land. Let me try a different approach. I'm not here to pretend your equipment isn't excellent. I'm here because your terms — payment schedule, warranty scope, training commitments, spare parts pricing — are structured for a buyer with no alternatives. I have alternatives. The question is whether you want to price like a monopoly or partner like a leader.
lines.tw: 歷史上可能是真的——但您應該知道我已經跟兩家台灣廠談過，他們換了而且留下來了。所以這個論點站不住。讓我換個角度。我不是來假裝您的設備不優秀。我來是因為**您的條款——付款排程、保固範圍、訓練承諾、備品定價——是為『沒有替代方案的買方』設計的**。**我有替代方案**。問題是您要**像壟斷者那樣定價，還是像領導者那樣合作**。

lines.en: Strong words. But you came to us, Mr. Lin — not the other way around. That tells me something about your real options.
lines.tw: 用詞很重。但是是您來找我們的，林先生——**不是我們找您**。**這告訴我關於您實際選項的一些事**。

lines.en: I came to you first because you are my preferred choice — and I want to be honest about that. But preferring you doesn't mean accepting any terms you offer. If anything, it means you owe me the better terms, because I'm walking away from a competing offer to be here. That's the cost of my preference. What's the cost of yours?
lines.tw: 我先來找您是因為您是我的首選——這點我願意誠實說。但**偏好您不代表接受您提出的任何條款**。**真要說，這代表您欠我更好的條款**，因為我為了來這裡放棄了一個競爭報價。**那是我的偏好的代價**。**您的偏好的代價是什麼？**

lines.en: What specifically are you asking to change?
lines.tw: 您具體要改什麼？

lines.en: Three things. One — payment terms: 30% on order, 40% on installation, 30% on acceptance. Not 50% upfront. You bear delivery risk, I bear performance risk — that's the right split. Two — warranty: 24 months full coverage, not 12. If your equipment is as reliable as you say, this costs you nothing — it just signals your confidence. Three — spare parts pricing: capped at 120% of OEM cost, not the current open-ended pricing. I'm not asking for charity. I'm asking for fairness.
lines.tw: 三件事。第一——**付款條件**：下單付 30%、安裝付 40%、驗收付 30%。**不是**預付 50%。**您承擔交貨風險，我承擔運轉風險——這是正確的拆分**。第二——**保固**：24 個月完整保固，不是 12 個月。如果您的設備像您說的那樣可靠，**這對您不花一毛——只是表達您的信心**。第三——**備品定價**：上限為**原廠成本的 120%**，不是現在這種無上限定價。**我不是要求慈善。我是要求公平**。

lines.en: Some of this we can discuss. But the 50% upfront — that is corporate policy, I cannot change it.
lines.tw: **有些**我們可以討論。但 50% 預付——**這是公司政策，我不能改**。

lines.en: I hear you on policy — and I respect that. But policy isn't physics. Policies get exceptions when the deal justifies it. So here's what I'll do. I'll accept 40% upfront — not 30%, not 50% — if you commit to a 60-day installation completion clause with daily penalties. You wanted me to share risk. Fine — let's both share it. Take that back to your principal and tell them I'm offering a structure where both sides have skin in the game. If they say no, I'll know it's not about policy.
lines.tw: 我聽到您說政策——我尊重這點。**但政策不是物理定律**。值得的案子，政策會有例外。**所以我這樣做**——我接受 40% 預付，不是 30%、不是 50%——**如果您承諾 60 天內完成安裝的條款，逾期每天罰款**。**您要我分擔風險，好——我們雙方都分擔**。**把這個帶回給您的主管，告訴他們我提出一個雙方都有 skin in the game 的結構**。**如果他們說不**，我就知道這不是政策問題。

lines.en: Final question. In one sentence — why should my CEO take this back to Seoul instead of standing firm?
lines.tw: 最後一個問題。用一句話——我的 CEO 為什麼該把這個帶回首爾，而不是堅持立場？

lines.en: Because the customer who pushes hardest in negotiation is usually the one who pays on time afterwards.
lines.tw: 因為談判時推得最用力的客戶，通常是事後最準時付款的那個。

lines.en: Mr. Lin — I'll call Seoul tonight. Don't go to my competitor yet.
lines.tw: 林先生——我今晚打給首爾。**先別去找我的競爭對手**。

episodes.title: Sell-Side: Chinese Procurement Compares You to Local Alternatives
episodes.tagline: 你是賣方 · 中國採購主管殺價 · 拿出本土競品報價 · 你要守住價值但不踩線

lines.en: Lin Zong, thank you for coming over. I'll switch to English so we're on the same page. Look — I'll be direct. We have three local Chinese suppliers quoting this certification service at roughly 60% of your price. They are responsive, technically competent, and culturally aligned with our team. Tell me why we should still work with you.
lines.tw: 林總，謝謝您過來。我切換成英文，這樣我們在同一個頻道。我直接點——**我們有三個本土中國供應商**報這個認證服務，價格大約是您的 60%。他們**回應快、技術能力夠、跟我們團隊文化也對得上**。**告訴我為什麼我們還要跟您合作**。

lines.en: I appreciate the straight talk — let me return it. If those three suppliers can deliver identical scope, with identical international recognition, at 60% of my price — you should work with them. I'm not here to argue you out of a better deal. What I am here to ensure is that you're comparing the same thing. Can I ask three questions?
lines.tw: 我欣賞您的直接——我用同樣方式回應。如果那三個供應商能交付**同樣範圍**、**同樣國際認可**、是我價格的 60%——**您應該跟他們合作**。我不是來說服您放棄更好的交易。**我來這裡確保的是**——您比較的是同一個東西。**我可以問三個問題嗎？**

lines.en: Go ahead. But understand — I've heard the 'international recognition' argument before. My buyers in Europe never ask whose name is on the certificate. They ask if the product works.
lines.tw: 問吧。但您要懂——**我聽過『國際認可』這個論點**。我在歐洲的買家從來不問憑證上是誰的名字。他們問產品 work 不 work。

lines.en: That may be true today. Three questions. One — when your European buyer's compliance team audits your supply chain next year under CSDDD or CBAM, will a local Chinese certificate satisfy them, or will they require recognized international body sign-off? Two — when a product issue arises and your buyer's legal team requests third-party technical liability documentation, can your local supplier indemnify you in a EU court? Three — if your local supplier's certification is questioned by an NGO or competitor, do you have a name your buyer already trusts, or do you have to defend an unknown one? Those are not 'international recognition' arguments — those are 'what happens when things go wrong' arguments.
lines.tw: 今天可能是真的。三個問題。**第一**——當您的歐洲買家明年根據 **CSDDD 或 CBAM** 稽核您的供應鏈時，本土中國憑證會通過嗎？還是他們會要求**被認可的國際機構簽字**？**第二**——當產品出問題、您買家的法務團隊要求**第三方技術責任文件**時，您的本土供應商**能在歐盟法院為您承擔責任嗎？第三**——如果您本土供應商的認證**被 NGO 或競爭對手質疑**，您是有一個**您買家已經信任的名字**，還是必須去捍衛一個沒人聽過的？**這些不是『國際認可』的論點——這些是『出事的時候會怎樣』的論點**。

lines.en: You're using fear to justify the price gap. The reality is — most issues never arise. Statistically, your scenarios are edge cases. Why should I pay a premium for low-probability events?
lines.tw: 您在用恐懼合理化價差。現實是——**大多數問題從來不會發生**。**統計上，您講的場景是邊緣案例**。**我為什麼要為低機率事件付溢價？**

lines.en: Fair challenge. Let me reframe. You're not paying for the probability of the event — you're paying for the cost when it happens. A 2% probability of a 5 million euro recall is mathematically the same as a 100% certainty of a 100,000 euro cost. Procurement teams who only optimize for unit price end up explaining to the CEO why the cheap supplier just cost the company a major customer. I'm not selling you fear. I'm selling you the math your CFO actually uses.
lines.tw: 公平的挑戰。讓我重新 frame。**您不是在為事件的機率付錢**——您是在為**事件發生時的成本**付錢。**2% 機率乘以 500 萬歐元召回，跟 100% 確定的 10 萬歐元成本，數學上是一樣的**。**只優化單價的採購團隊**最後會跟 CEO 解釋為什麼便宜的供應商剛剛害公司丟了一個重要客戶。**我不是賣您恐懼。我是賣您 CFO 真正用的數學**。

lines.en: That's clever framing. But I need to take something back to my director. Can you reduce your price by 20%? Anything less and I cannot justify keeping you over the local options.
lines.tw: 包裝得很巧妙。但我需要拿東西回去給我主管。**您能降 20% 嗎？**少於這個我沒辦法說服我主管選您而不選本土選項。

lines.en: I won't reduce my list price by 20% — and let me explain why that's actually a sign of respect for you, not the opposite. If I drop 20% in a single meeting, your director will rightly ask: 'why was he charging us 20% more last week?' You will look bad, not good. What I will do — I'll keep the price, and add scope worth approximately 18% of contract value at no charge: annual surveillance audit, two emergency response visits, training for your QA team. Your director sees enhanced value, not panic pricing. You get more for the same budget. That story works inside your company.
lines.tw: 我不會把標價降 20%——讓我解釋為什麼這其實是對您的尊重，而不是相反。**如果我一次會議降 20%**，您主管會合理地問：『他上週為什麼比現在貴 20%？』**您會看起來糟，不是好**。**我會做的是**——維持價格，加入**價值約合約 18% 的附加範圍、免費**：**年度監督稽核、兩次緊急回應出勤、您 QA 團隊的訓練**。**您主管看到的是強化價值，不是恐慌定價**。**您用同樣預算拿到更多**。**那個故事在您公司內部是 work 的**。

lines.en: The added scope is interesting. But my director will still ask — why not just take the 60% local quote and self-insure the risk? We have the balance sheet for it. What's your answer to that?
lines.tw: 加的 scope 有趣。但我主管還是會問——**為什麼不直接拿本土 60% 的報價、風險自己承擔？**我們有資產負債表撐得起。**您怎麼回應這個？**

lines.en: Self-insuring is a CFO fantasy that doesn't survive contact with reality. Here's why. If a recall happens, your balance sheet covers the cash cost — but it doesn't cover the customer who switches to your competitor, the regulator who flags you for re-audit, or the investor call where you explain why you went cheap. Those costs don't show up in any insurance line item — but they're the ones that actually break companies. You're not buying my certificate. You're buying the absence of those conversations.
lines.tw: 自我承擔風險是 CFO 的幻想，碰到現實就破了。**原因在這裡**。如果發生召回，您的資產負債表 cover 現金成本——但它 cover 不了**轉到您競爭對手的客戶**、**標您去重新稽核的監管機構**、或**您要解釋為什麼選便宜的投資人電話會議**。**這些成本不會出現在任何保險項目裡——但它們才是真正搞垮公司的**。您不是在買我的憑證。**您是在買『那些對話不會發生』**。

lines.en: Final question. In one sentence — why should a Chinese company in 2026 still pay an international premium?
lines.tw: 最後一個問題。用一句話——一家 2026 年的中國公司為什麼還要付國際溢價？

lines.en: Because your customers in Europe and the US are not buying your product — they're buying your supply chain.
lines.tw: 因為您在歐洲跟美國的客戶不是在買您的產品——他們是在買您的供應鏈。

lines.en: Lin Zong — that is a sentence I will quote internally. Let me speak with my director and come back to you.
lines.tw: 林總——這句話我會在內部引用。**讓我跟我主管談談再回覆您**。
